exemplar.title: Effects of images (non-verbal representations) in the recalling of information

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear statement and explanation of aim and relevance linked to Dual Coding Theory
Detailed description of Paivioâs model and explicit connection to the manipulation
Operationalized hypothesis with identified IV and DV
weaknesses: Incomplete summary of Bablekou et al. (2003) study leaves context underdeveloped
Minor imprecision in phrasing of the null hypothesis
Lack of broader societal applications limits depth of relevance
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the introduction is well-structured and clearly ties the aim to Dual Coding Theory, omissions in the literature summary and minor imprecisions prevent full marks. The aim, theory description and variable operationalization are all solid (band 5â6 evidence), but missing context and slight wording issues justify a 4.

strengths: Research design choice is explained with reference to practice effects and demand characteristics
Sampling technique is justified in terms of recruitment ease and language control
Controlled variables and material choices are clearly listed and rationalized
weaknesses: A few typographical errors (e.g. âto main uniformityâ) momentarily hinder clarity
Volunteer sampling rationale could be deepened with discussion of potential biases
Procedure description, while detailed, occasionally uses ambiguous phrasing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a strong grasp of design, sampling and control (3â4 band), but minor language slips and missed opportunities to discuss bias nuances limit the award to the mid-band. A score of 3 reflects solid explanation with room for clearer expression.

strengths: Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (MannâWhitney U) statistics applied correctly
Graphical presentation addresses the hypothesis with labeled axes and condition means
Findings are interpreted in relation to the data and linked back to the hypothesis and theory
weaknesses: Inconsistencies in SD reporting between table and text
Graph lacks a clear y-axis label for âmean number of facts recalledâ
Mixed decimal notation detracts from professional presentation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical methods and interpretation meet the criteria for band 5â6, but reporting inconsistencies and presentation errors keep the work from perfection. A score of 4 acknowledges accurate analysis while noting these technical issues.

strengths: Strengths and limitations are relevant and well explained in relation to validity
Modifications are explicitly linked to each limitation and justified
Findings are connected to Dual Coding Theory and compared with prior research
weaknesses: Discussion of findings remains descriptive and lacks alternative theoretical perspectives
Evaluation does not fully explore wider implications or counter-arguments
Minor statistical reporting errors appear in the conclusion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation shows clear identification of limitations and justified improvements (band 5â6 evidence) but the discussion of findings is one-sided and lacks critical depth. A band 3â4 performance is evident, warranting a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with labelled axes, title, condition names and mean values directly addresses the hypothesis.  The technique is appropriate and accurately plotted.  Small presentation issues (decimal commas, cropped y-axis in screenshot) are minor, so the graph fits the 5-6 band at 5.

The student states statistical outcomes, rejects the null, and explicitly links the higher recall in the image-text condition to Dual Coding Theory and the original hypothesis.  Interpretation is coherent and grounded in the data.  Some repetition limits depth, yielding 5 rather than 6.

A clear research (directional) hypothesis and a null hypothesis are provided.  The IV (presentation mode: image-text vs text-text) and DV (number of facts correctly recalled) are not only identified but also operationalised through specific materials and recall scoring.  This fulfils the highest descriptor; minor wording imprecision ("amount of facts") keeps the award at 5 rather than 6.

Volunteer sampling is identified, participant characteristics (age, language, school type) are given, and the choice is justified (ease of recruitment, controlling for language proficiency).  Random allocation to conditions is also mentioned.  These elements constitute an explanation, placing the work firmly in the 3-4 range; comprehensiveness supports a 4.

Dual Coding Theory is described in sufficient detail (two subsystems, associative processing, parallel encoding) and specific features are cited from Paivio (1991).  The student then explicitly connects these theoretical claims to the present manipulation (pictures + text versus text alone).  Because both description and connection are present and accurate, the strand meets the 5-6 band, though a deeper critique of the model is absent, so 5 not 6.

Findings are related to Dual Coding Theory and compared with the original study, but the discussion remains relatively descriptive and one-sided; alternative explanations or wider theoretical implications are not developed.  This meets the 3-4 descriptor but lacks the breadth and depth for full âdiscussedâ, so 3.

The student clearly states the investigationâs aim â to test whether image-text pairs improve factual recall compared with text-text pairs among high-school students â and explicitly situates this aim within Paivioâs Dual Coding Theory, explaining why the issue is educationally relevant.  The explanation of relevance (activation of both verbal and non-verbal subsystems enhances memory) goes beyond mere identification, so the work fits the 5-6 descriptor.  Minor brevity in linking to wider societal applications prevents a full 6.

Multiple strengths and limitations across design, sampling, procedure and materials are identified and each is explained in relation to potential impact on validity.  The explanations are relevant and specific, satisfying the top band; slight unevenness in depth keeps the score at 5.

Controlled variables (timing, font, neutral stimuli, classroom separation) are listed and reasons for each control are provided, evidencing explanation rather than mere description.  Materials are justified (fungi chosen to minimise cultural bias), satisfying the top of the 3-4 band.

For every limitation mentioned, a concrete, justified modification is proposed (pre-test memory ability, restrict to native speakers, extend writing time).  The links between limitation and improvement are explicit, fulfilling the highest descriptor; justification is clear though concise, earning 5.

The student explains that an independent-measures design was chosen to prevent practice/order effects and demand characteristics, demonstrating understanding, not simple description.  Rationale is succinct but present, satisfying the explanation requirement in the 3-4 band; clarity and depth merit the top of that band.

Descriptive statistics (mean, SD) and an appropriate non-parametric inferential test (Mann-Whitney U) are correctly applied to independent groups with small N.  Calculations appear accurate and p-value interpretation is correct.  Minor inconsistencies in reporting SD ("2.7") prevent a flawless 6 but the work meets the accuracy threshold for band 5-6, scored 5.

annotations.comment: The dependent variable is described as the âamount of facts recalledâ; operationalize this more precisely (e.g. ânumber of facts correctly listedâ) to avoid ambiguity.
The null hypothesis is stated appropriately, but it could be phrased more formally (e.g. âno significant difference between conditionsâ).
The rationale for using an independentâmeasures design is well explained, linking avoidance of practice effects to methodological rigor.
Volunteer sampling is justified by ease of recruitment and control of language proficiency, reflecting thoughtful sampling choices.
Controlled variables and material choices (fungi to avoid cultural bias) are well explained, demonstrating thorough experimental control.
Minor typo: âto main uniformityâ should read âto maintain uniformity.â Ensuring clear language strengthens procedural clarity.
The procedure description is detailed, specifying timing and recall tasks clearly, which enhances reproducibility.
Ethical measures (consent, debriefing, anonymity) are appropriately described and adhere to standard protocols.
Table 1 correctly presents means and standard deviations for both groups, facilitating transparent comparison.
Interpretation of descriptive results precedes inferential statistics; consider summarizing Mann-Whitney results immediately after descriptive analysis to strengthen argumentative flow.
The text states an SD of 2.7, yet the table lists 2.26 and 3.59; reconcile these values to ensure internal consistency.
The student provides a clear and accurate description of Paivioâs Dual Coding Theory, citing its verbal and nonâverbal subsystems in detail, which effectively grounds the investigation in theory.
While the theory is summarized well, consider discussing potential limitations or critiques of Dual Coding Theory to demonstrate critical engagement with the model.
The description of the Bablekou et al. (2003) study ends midâsentence, making the context and relevance unclear; complete this summary and explicitly link its findings to your investigation.
The aim is articulated clearly and directly tied to activating both subsystems for factual recall, demonstrating strong relevance to Dual Coding Theory.
The bar chart uses an appropriate format but lacks a yâaxis label indicating âMean number of facts recalledâ; add this label for clarity.
Decimal commas are mixed with points in pâvalue reporting (e.g. â0,05â vs â0.0007â); standardize to one format (preferably point notation) throughout.
The final interpretation restates earlier findings rather than extending insight; consider integrating inferential results and theoretical implications here.
Discussion links findings to Dual Coding Theory but omits alternative models (e.g. Levels of Processing); broadening theoretical comparison would deepen evaluation.
Identifying participantsâ baseline memory ability as a limitation is well done; the proposed preâtest directly addresses disparities in recall performance.
Proposed restriction to native English speakers strengthens internal validity; consider also matching participants by level of instruction to reduce further variability.
Allowing unlimited recall time addresses writingâspeed bias but should explicitly state how this links to enhancing measurement of memory rather than penmanship.
The suggestion to equalize fungus name lengths is insightful; you could pilot different lengths to quantify its impact on recall speed.
Conclusion correctly rejects the null but reports p=0.007 instead of 0.0007; ensure consistency in inferential statistics.
Enhance the conclusion by discussing realâworld implications for educational practice, such as designing multimedia learning materials.
